Off Calendar Apr-04-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Jimmie L. Williams, Esq., Heartsyugker et al, 510-457-4400, Intervenor for Farmers Insurance;

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT FARMERS INSURANCE EXCHANGE Motion For Leave To Intervene

GRANTED. No opposition filed. "The purpose of intervention is to promote fairness by involving all parties potentially affected by a judgment in the litigation." (Simpson Redwood Co. v. State of California (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 1192, 1199 [citation omitted].) As an insurer for Defendant Yasinia Kennedy, Farmers has a direct interest in this litigation and a sound basis to intervene to defend against default on Ms. Kennedy's behalf under Code of Civil Procedure section 387 and Insurance Code section 11580. "[W]here the insurer may be subject to a direct action under Insurance Code section 11580 by a judgment creditor who has or will obtain a default judgment in a third party action against the insured, intervention is appropriate." (Reliance Insurance Company v. Superior Court (2002) 84 Cal.App.4th 383,

Order is signed

Notice Of Motion To Quash Plaintiffs Service By Publication Of Defendant Yasinia Kennedy
Off Calendar Apr-04-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Jimmie L. Williams, Esq., Heartsyugker et al, 510-457-4400, Intervenor for Farmers Insurance;

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT YASINIA KENNEDY'S Motion To Quash Plaintiffs Service By Publication

Intervenor Farmers Insurance Exchange's motion to quash Plaintiffs Gloria Lee and David Greenberg's service by publication of Defendant Yasinia Kennedy is denied. In its October 12, 2018 Order, the Court found that Defendant Yasinia Kennedy could not be served with reasonable diligence by any means other than publication. (Code Civ. Proc. 415.50(a)(1).) The Order authorized Plaintiffs to serve Ms. Kennedy by publication in the East Bay Express Newspaper and in the Las Vegas Review Journal. Intervenor has not provided any basis for the Court to question that prior order. (See also Greene v. State Farm Fire & Casualty Co. (1990) 224 Cal.App.3d 1583, 1588 [the power of one judge to vacate an order duly made by another judge is limited].) Thus, Plaintiffs properly served Ms. Kennedy by publication as authorized by the October 12, 2018 Order.

In opposing Intervenor's motion, Plaintiffs have provided evidence that they published the service in each of these publications once a week for four successive weeks. (Albini Decl., Exhs. B, C.) They have, therefore, complied with the Order and with the requirements for service by publication under Code of Civil Procedure section 415.50. Counsel for Plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and that complies with CRC 3.1312

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication Against Plaintiffs
Off Calendar Feb-05-2018 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Bradford A. Rosenblatt, Esq. of Wiggins Marks Rosenblatt LLP, T:415.840.7177 x210, for defendant/moving party;
Emile A. Davise, Esq. and Megan R. Irish, Esq. of the Dolan Law Firm, T:415.421.2800, for plaintiff/opposing party.

RULING: A R G U E D. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopts the tentative ruling. DEFENDANTS MEGABUS WEST, LLC's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT And Alternative MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION on the complaint filed by plaintiffs Gloria Lee and David Greenberg are denied. Assuming that Megabus sustained its initial burden of production, there is a triable dispute whether Megabus was defendant Chyna Kennedy's special or dual employer and thus vicariously liable for plaintiffs' injuries. (Riley v. Southwest Marine, Inc., (1988) 203 Cal.App.3d 1242, 1250.) The evidence submitted by plaintiff creating this triable dispute includes: (1) page 2 of the agreement between Megabus West and Discovery Management, LLC, which allows Megabus to remove a porter at any time and without stating a reason; (2) Ms. Kennedy was performing Megabus' work (Waters Depo., 79:5-80:8; 90:20-92:12); (3) MegaBus exerted substantial control over Ms. Kennedy that included determining when she worked and whether she could work for Megabus at all (Waters Depo., 80:18-82:1; 88:8-89:19; (4) Megabus provided Ms. Kennedy's tools, training, and uniform (Waters Depo., 55:23-56:5; 72:5-73:20; 92:19-93:12; 104:14-21); and (5) Ms. Kennedy's work was not skilled and she did not have operational control over the details. Megabus' argument raised for the first time in its reply brief that Ms. Kennedy was not acting within the course and scope of her employment while using her vehicle was not considered by the court. The moving papers, including Megabus' separate statement of undisputed facts, do not raise this issue and thus it may not be grounds to grant the motion.

ORDER SIGNED. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: G. Gonzales; Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR#8600, email: torreanom@aol.com; mtorreano@sftc.org.
